pick: every minute of the midnight hour
reasoning: "The midnight hour" is a fixed English idiom unambiguously denoting 00:00–00:59. The range form "from midnight through 12:59 a.m." is technically correct but awkward: midnight IS 12:00 a.m., so naming both the anchor and the endpoint in the same 12-hour zone invites the question of whether 12:00 a.m. itself is included before "12:59 a.m." — a subtle confusion the idiom avoids entirely.

pick: every minute of the noon hour
reasoning: "The noon hour" is a well-established idiom for 12:00–12:59 p.m. The range form "from noon through 12:59 p.m." is redundant (noon = 12:00 p.m.) and the double 12-reference in a single phrase can momentarily mislead a reader about which clock-hour is meant. The idiom is shorter and carries no ambiguity.

pick: every minute of the 9 a.m. hour
reasoning: Both candidates are clear for h9. The idiom form wins marginally: "the 9 a.m. hour" is standard English for the hour-block starting at 09:00, there is no plausible misreading as "9 minutes" given the surrounding words "every minute of", and it is more concise. The range form "from 9 a.m. through 9:59 a.m." is equally correct but the repeated "a.m." is slightly redundant. Confidence 4 (not 5) because the idiom pattern is less entrenched for arbitrary hours than for midnight/noon.

pick: every minute of the 5 p.m. hour
reasoning: Same reasoning as h9. "The 5 p.m. hour" straightforwardly names the hour-block starting at 17:00; no endpoint-exclusion risk, no ambiguity about which 60-minute span is meant. The range form "from 5 p.m. through 5:59 p.m." is correct but the repeated "p.m." adds visual noise without clarity gain. Confidence 4 for the same reason as h9.
